- Target latency SLO: p95 < 120ms for short chat turns (~64 tokens)
- Long-form background generation: p95 < 800ms for 512-token responses
- Cost sensitivity: prefer tests that reflect tokens/sec and per-token cost at 95th percentile

Can you confirm whether anything changed on your side (cache settings, model variant routing, prefix invalidation)? This is impacting checkout flows. We need an ETA and mitigation plan.

Hey Ben,

Re-attaching minimal repro logs and a short video clip.

Questions: is there a known limit on streaming/session length? Is there a recommended client-side retry pattern we should use to avoid partial outputs? We're evaluating Redwood for a critical feature so any guidance helps.

Head of Product, VelvetHost

Thanks for the productive call earlier today. Quick recap and a few follow-ups so we can finalize the SLO wording for the technical and legal teams:

- Desired targets we discussed:
 • Streaming: time-to-first-byte (TTFB) p95 <= 150ms; p99 <= 400ms
 • Non-streaming (complete response): p95 <= 350ms; p99 <= 900ms
 • Throughput: sustain 50 req/s steady-state with bursts to 120 req/s for short windows
 • Concurrency: support 100 concurrent open WebSocket streams without queueing

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>

Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-12T09:14:00Z
Subject: Kite-XS variant: compatibility summary + ask for temporary pin

Team —

We just got the vendor compatibility report from Nimbus for the Kite-XS 4B family (see attached). High level: the model appears to run fine in our runtime, but there are two areas where the variant diverges from our standard tokenizer and KV layout which could cause downstream hallucination/regression for a subset of prompt shapes.

Quick summary: 
- Tokenization differences: subtle BOS/EOS treatment on edge-case punctuation sequences.
- KV cache ordering: variant uses packed KV dims which changes memory layout assumptions in a few lower-level kernels.

Ask: before we let this variant into the public catalog, I want ...

Subject: Realtime: Hosted API latency spike impacting Furnishly checkout

Hi Lauren,

We're seeing a sharp regression in p95/p99 latency on the Hosted API that's spiking our checkout times and causing payment timeouts. Impact started ~08:52 PDT. Quick numbers from our monitoring: p95 went from ~120ms to ~1.1s, p99 from ~240ms to ~2.8s, error rate jumped from 0.2% to ~2.4% over the last 15 minutes. Throughput for our integration has dipped ~18% as requests back off.

Sample request id: req_9f3b2a (from 2026-08-17T08:54:12Z). We can reproduce on multiple regions (us-west1 primarily). This is customer-facing and blocking checkout flows for ~6% of sessions.

We urgently need a triage and a mitigation plan. Attaching a small snippet of our request logs and a synthetic tra...

Subject: Re: Commit tiers and guardrails — per-token pricing / price protections (ACME)

Thanks for the demo today, Ben. We like Redwood's latency profile and the ability to pin model versions. Before we move to pricing approvals I need clarity on a couple of protection items ACME legal/finance care about:

- Can you commit to a 12-month price lock on per-token rates for committed tiers?
- Does Redwood offer an MFN clause (i.e., will ACME be protected if you later publish a lower per-token rate to another customer)?
- We have an internal policy that annual increases tied to inflation must be capped; are you able to support a CPI cap (we typically see requests for a 3% cap).

Specifically: a commitment around sustained tokens/sec for steady workloads and explicit headroom rules for preemption/eviction during multi-tenant bursts.

- Guarantees 95th-percentile steady throughput at a committed concurrency profile (attached).
- Defines preemption windows and a 72-hr notice for planned maintenance...
